en: All right, let's put our historian hat back on. Okay. Will the American empire collapse one day?
ko: 좋아요, 역사학자 모자를 다시 써봐요. 미국 제국도 언젠가 붕괴할까요?

en: And if it does, when it does, what will be the reason? Statistically, likely.
ko: 만약 그렇다면, 언제 그리고 이유가 뭘까요? 통계적으로 가능성이 높아요.

en: The, statistically, yes. You know, it's the famous Fight Club quote, it's like on a long enough timeline, the survival rate for everything drops to zero.
ko: 통계적으로 네. 유명한 파이트 클럽 대사처럼, 충분히 긴 시간선에서 모든 것의 생존률은 0으로 떨어지니까요.

en: And, you know. I like for all the books you've quoted, you went to Fight Club, I guess the movie, right?
ko: 인용하신 모든 책 중에 파이트 클럽이라니. 영화죠? 책도 좋으니까 읽어보세요.

en: The book is good though, people should read that too. In terms of why, again, statistically, the answer's quite simple.
ko: 왜냐에 대해서는 통계적으로 답이 간단해요.

en: It usually comes back to a series of unpopular wars, which are pursued because of the elite's interests.
ko: 보통 엘리트의 이익 때문에 추진되는 일련의 인기 없는 전쟁으로 돌아와요.

en: Then it usually leads to a miscalculation and not a catastrophic defeat normally, it comes gradually.
ko: 보통 오판으로 이어지는데 대참패가 아니라 점진적으로 와요.

en: And most of the times when these things end, the crazy part is most people who are living through end of empire have no idea that they're living through the end of the empire.
ko: 미친 건 제국의 끝을 살아가는 대부분의 사람들이 그걸 모른다는 거예요.

en: And I actually think about that a lot from, you know, Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire by Edward Gibbon.
ko: 에드워드 기번의 로마 제국 쇠망사를 많이 생각해요.

en: Actually, your episode on Rome was fantastic. People should go listen to that. So there you go. Another really good one, I like to think a lot about the British empire and what eventually led to that collapse.
ko: 당신의 로마 에피소드가 환상적이었어요. 꼭 들어보세요. 대영제국과 그 붕괴에 대해서도 많이 생각해요.

en: And nobody in 1919 said the British empire has collapsed. Basically, nobody thought that.
ko: 1919년에 대영제국이 붕괴했다고 말한 사람은 아무도 없었어요.

en: They were like, yeah, the First World War is horrible, but actually we came out of this okay, we still have India, you know, we still have all these African colonies and all that.
ko: 1차 대전은 끔찍했지만 인도도 있고 아프리카 식민지도 있으니 괜찮다고 생각했어요.

en: But, you know, long periods of servitude, of debt to the United States, of degradation, of social upheaval, of Bolshevism, of American industrial might.
ko: 하지만 미국에 대한 오랜 부채, 퇴보, 사회적 격변, 볼셰비즘, 미국의 산업력.

en: And next thing you know, you find yourself at Potsdam and Churchill's like, holy shit, I have barely any power in this room, right?
ko: 어느새 포츠담에 가있고 처칠이 이 방에서 거의 권력이 없다는 걸 깨닫는 거예요.

en: So revolutions happen slowly and then all at once. And so could you really put a, you know, a real like pin in the end of the British empire?
ko: 혁명은 천천히, 그리고 한꺼번에 일어나죠. 대영제국의 끝에 핀을 꽂을 수 있나요?

en: It took almost 40 years for it to end. So America's empire will eventually end either from rising geopolitical competition, likely China, could be India, nobody knows.
ko: 끝나는 데 거의 40년이 걸렸어요. 미국의 제국도 결국 끝날 거예요. 지정학적 경쟁, 아마 중국, 인도일 수도 있고.

en: It will likely be because of being overstretched of an elite capture is usually the reason why, and a misreading of what made your original society work, you know, in the first place.
ko: 과도한 확장, 엘리트 포획, 원래 사회를 작동하게 한 것에 대한 오독 때문이겠죠.

en: And that is one where I honestly, like all three of those things will happen all at once and it will happen over an extremely long period of time.
ko: 세 가지가 동시에 일어나고 매우 오랜 기간에 걸칠 거예요.

en: And it's very difficult to predict. I would not bet against America right now. I think we have a lot of fundamental strengths.
ko: 예측하기 매우 어렵고. 지금 미국에 반대로 베팅하지 않겠어요. 근본적 강점이 많으니까요.

en: It's such a unique and dynamic country. It really is fucking crazy.
ko: 정말 독특하고 역동적인 나라예요.

en: Every time I travel the world, as much as I love all these different places, I go, man, I just, I love the United States so much.
ko: 세계를 여행할 때마다 미국을 너무 사랑한다는 걸 느껴요.

en: You will love it more when you leave. I really believe that, so.
ko: 떠나면 더 사랑하게 돼요. 정말 그래요.

en: Yeah, and it's nice to remember how quickly the public opinion shifts, like we're very dynamic and adaptable, which annoys me.
ko: 여론이 얼마나 빨리 바뀌는지 기억하면 좋아요. 매우 역동적이고 적응력이 있으니까.

en: I understand that's part of the political discourse saying like, if Trump wins, it's the end of America. If Kamala wins, it's the end of America.
ko: 트럼프가 이기면 미국의 끝, 카말라가 이기면 미국의 끝. 정치 담론의 일부인 건 알지만.

en: So stupid. Yeah. But I understand that the radical nature of that discourse is necessary to, like we mentioned.
ko: 바보 같아요. 하지만 급진적 담론이 투표를 이끌어내는 데 필요하다는 건 이해해요.

en: Yeah, to drive out votes. To drive up votes. I like to think about Americans in 1866.
ko: 투표를 끌어내려고. 1866년의 미국인들을 생각해봐요.

en: I cannot imagine going through a war where some X percent, I think it was like two or 3% or whatever, the entire population was just killed.
ko: 전체 인구의 2~3%가 죽은 전쟁을 겪는 걸 상상할 수 없어요.

en: Our president, who was this visionary genius who we were blessed to have, is assassinated at Forks Theater immediately after the surrender of Lee.
ko: 비전 있는 천재였던 대통령이 리의 항복 직후 암살당했어요.

en: Andrew Johnson, who's a bumbling like fucktard, is the one who is in charge.
ko: 무능한 앤드루 존슨이 책임자가 됐고.

en: And, you know, we're having all these insane crises over like internal management while we're also trying to decide like this new order in the South and whether to bring these people, how to bring these people back into the union.
ko: 남부의 새 질서와 이 사람들을 어떻게 연방에 복귀시킬지 결정하면서 온갖 위기를 겪고 있었어요.

en: I mean, I would have despaired in that year. I was like, it's over, this is it. You know, the war, I'd be like, was it worth anything?
ko: 그 해에 절망했을 거예요. 끝났다, 전쟁이 무슨 의미가 있었나.

en: You know, if Andrew Johnson is gonna be doing this, or even in the South, I mean, I can't even imagine for what they were going through too.
ko: 앤드루 존슨이 이런다면, 남부도 도시가 다 불탔고 경제와 생활 방식이 5년 만에 전복됐으니까.

en: You know, they have to go home and their entire cities are burned to the ground and they're trying to readjust and their entire economy and way of life is overthrown in five years.
ko: 미친 시대인데 결국 잘 됐잖아요.

en: I mean, that's an insane time to be alive. And what do we know? It worked out, you know.
ko: 1890년대쯤에는 악수하고 있었어요. FDR이 마지막 게티즈버그 참전용사들에게 연설하는 유튜브 영상이 있어요.

en: By 1890s or so, there were people shaking hands, you know, union, and there's a cool video on YouTube actually of FDR who is addressing some of the last Gettysburg veterans.
ko: 75주년 기념인 것 같은데, 돌담을 사이에 두고 악수하는 노인들을 볼 수 있어요.

en: I think it was like the 75th anniversary or whatever. And you can literally see these old men who are shaking hands across the stone wall.
ko: 희망을 줘요. 그 희망에 머물러봐요.

en: It gives me hope, yeah. Let's linger on that hope.
ko: 21세기, 그 이후, 인류 문명 전반에 대한 낙관의 근거는 뭔가요?

en: What is the source of optimism you have for the 21st century, for the century beyond that, for human civilization in general?
ko: 역사에서 냉소적 교훈을 배우기 쉽잖아요. 결국 잘못되는 거.

en: Is, you know, it's easy to learn cynical lessons from history, right? That shit eventually goes wrong.
ko: 하지만 가끔은 안 그래요. 뭐가 희망을 주나요?

en: But sometimes it doesn't. So what gives you hope?
ko: 인류를 위대하게 만드는 근본이 미국적 특성에 가장 잘 표현된다고 봐요.

en: I think that the fundamentals of what makes humanity great and has for a long time are best expressed in the American character.
ko: 모든 문제에도 불구하고, 개인주의, 개척 정신, 지리적 축복, 경제적 축복, 다른 문화를 흡수하는 능력이 성공할 최고의 기회를 줘요.

en: And that despite all of our problems, that as a country with our ethos, a lot of stuff we talked about today, individualism, the frontier mindset, the blessings of geography, the blessings of our economy, of the way that we're able to just incorporate different cultures and the best of each and put them all together, give us the best opportunity to succeed and to accomplish awesome things.
ko: 달에 사람을 보낸 나라예요. 인간 정신의 정수죠.

en: We're the country that put a man on the moon, which is the epitome of human spirit. I hope to see more of that.
ko: 남극 탐험을 좋아해요. 남극에 대한 거의 모든 책을 읽었어요.

en: And, you know, I think last time I was here, I shouted out, and I love Antarctic exploration. I've read basically every book that there is on the exploration of Antarctica.
ko: 좋아하는 이유는 남극에 신경 쓸 이유가 전혀 없기 때문이에요.

en: And one of the reasons I love to do so is because there is no reason to care about Antarctica, none.
ko: 거기엔 아무것도 없어요. 남극점에 가는 건 진짜 쓸모없는 일이에요.

en: There's nothing down there, zero. Going to the South Pole is a truly useless exercise.
ko: 그런데도 갔어요. 두 번이나. 5주 간격으로 경쟁하면서.

en: And yet, we went. We went twice, actually. Two people went there within the span of five weeks and they competed to do so.
ko: 아문센과 스콧의 탐험을 추진한 정신, 특히 셰클턴, 제 모든 영웅 중의 영웅인데, 그 정신이 미국에 살아있다고 봐요.

en: And the spirit that propelled Amundsen and Scott's expedition and people like Shackleton, who is like, if you were to ask me, my hero of all heroes, it's Ernest Shackleton, is because his spirit, I think, lives on in the United States.
ko: 안타깝게도 영국에서는 죽었어요. 영국인들도 그걸 알아요.

en: It unfortunately died in Great Britain. And interestingly enough, the Brits even understand that.
ko: 셰클턴이 미국에서 얼마나 인기 있는지 흥미롭다고 하죠. 아일랜드인이고 영국 국민이지만 제게는 영적 미국인이에요.

en: They're like, it's very interesting how popular Shackleton is in America. And even though he was Irish and he was a British subject, to me, he's a spiritual American.
ko: 그의 정신이 우리 안에 살아있고 항상 어느 정도 있었다고 봐요.

en: And I think that his spirit lives on within us and has always been here to a certain extent.
ko: 다른 곳에서는 죽어가고 있지만, 여기가 좋아요. 미국의 멋진 점이 너무 많아요.

en: And everywhere else, I think it's dying. But here, I love it here. There's so many cool things about America.
ko: 사람들이 항상 이사하고, 새 집 사고, 가정을 꾸리고. 같은 나라에서 인생을 다시 시작할 수 있는 곳은 여기뿐이에요.

en: People move around all the time. They buy new houses. They start families. There's no other place you can just reset your whole life.
ko: 자신을 재발명할 수 있어요. 파산했다가 부자가 됐다가 여러 번 왔다 갔다 할 수 있어요.

en: In the same country, it's wild. You can reinvent yourself. You can go broke. You can get rich. You can go back and forth multiple times.
ko: 그 자유와 기회를 추구할 수 있는 곳은 여기뿐이에요.

en: And there's nowhere else where you have enough freedom and opportunity to pursue that.
ko: 문제는 많지만 세계를 충분히 여행해봤으니 특별한 곳이라는 걸 알아요.

en: And we definitely have a lot of problems, but I've traveled enough of the world now to know that it's a special place.
ko: 희망을 많이 줘요. 보스턴 억양으로, 쉬워서가 아니라 어렵기 때문에 이걸 합니다.

en: And that gives me a lot of hope. I wish I could do a Bostonian accent of, we do these things, not because they're easy, but because they're hard.
ko: 고마워요. 정말 그래요. 스코트-아일랜드계가 우리를 만들었네요.

en: Thank you. Oh, that's so true. The Scott Irish got us.
ko: 당신의 여정의 큰 팬이에요. 백악관에서 대통령을 인터뷰하는 모습을 보고 싶어요.

en: Well, listen, I'm a huge fan of your saga. I hope to see you in the White House interviewing the president.
ko: 그래요. 그게 제가 백악관에 갈 유일한 상황이에요. 앞줄에서 자유롭게 말하는 거.

en: There you go. That's the only situation you're gonna see me in the White House. Yeah. Front row and just talking free.
ko: 당신이 대변인이나 대통령과 대화하는 나라와 세계에서 살고 싶어요.

en: I would love to live in a country and in a world where it's you who gets to talk to the press secretary, to the president.
ko: 당신은 진짜 좋은 사람이에요. 언론인으로서도 인간으로서도.

en: Because I think you're a real, you're one of the good ones as far as journalists go, as far as human beings.
ko: 거기에 가시길 바라요. 그리고 질문을 하시길- 책에 실리는.

en: So I hope to see you in there. And I hope you get to ask a question that- That ends up in a book.
ko: 좋은 역사책에 실리는 질문을. 절대적으로. 저도 큰 팬이에요.

en: That ends up in a good history book. Absolutely. Well, likewise, I'm a huge fan of yours. For anybody out there who's interested, I compiled a list and I will go and retroactively edit it.
Just go to sagaandjeddy.io. I created a newsletter with a website that has all the links to all the books I'm gonna talk about here.
Beautiful. The hundreds of books that were mentioned here. All right, brother. Thank you so much for talking to me. Thank you.
